name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنْهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥١)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies: they are allies of each other. Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them. Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (51)
```

```arabic
فَتَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِيهِمْ يَقُولُونَ نَخْشَىٰٓ أَن تُصِيبَنَا دَآئِرَةٌ ۚ فَعَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِٱلْفَتْحِ أَوْ أَمْرٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ فَيُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَسَرُّوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٥٢)
```

```english
Yet you see those in whose hearts is a sickness rushing to them, saying, ‘We fear lest a turn of fortune should visit us.’ Maybe Allah will bring about a victory, or a command from Him, and then they will be regretful for what they kept secret in their hearts, (52)
```

```arabic
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۙ إِنَّهُمْ لَمَعَكُمْ ۚ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (٥٣)
```

```english
and the faithful will say, ‘Are these the ones who swore by Allah with solemn oaths that they were with you?!’ Their works have failed, and they have become losers. (53)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِى ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُۥٓ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ يُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَخَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لَآئِمٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٥٤)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, stern towards the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer. That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (54)
```

## (Commentary)

A general survey of these verses necessitates a pause regarding the connection of these verses to what precedes them, as well as the connection of what follows them—such as His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ** (Only Allah is your guardian and His Apostle...) [Qarai 5:55] until the end of the two verses—and then the connection of His saying after them: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَكُمْ هُزُواً** (O you who have faith! Do not take those who take your religion in mockery...) [Qarai 5:57] through the completion of several verses, and finally the connection of His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ** (O Apostle! Communicate...) [Qarai 5:67].

As for these four verses, they mention the Jews and the Christians. The Quran would not mention their affairs in its Meccan verses due to the lack of pressing need for it at that time; rather, it addresses their condition in the verses revealed in Medina. Nor does it address them in the verses revealed in the early period of the Migration (الهجرة), for the Muslims at that time were only afflicted by mixing and social interaction with the Jews, or making peace treaties with them, or specifically repelling their plots and deception, to the exclusion of the Christians—except in the latter half of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stay in Medina. Perhaps these four verses were revealed during that period, and perhaps the "victory" (الفتح) mentioned therein refers to the Conquest of Mecca.

However, it has been previously established that the reliance is upon the Surah al-Ma'idah being revealed in the year of the Farewell Pilgrimage (حجة الوداع), by which time Mecca had already been conquered. Is the intended "victory" then a victory other than the Conquest of Mecca? Or were these verses revealed before the Conquest of Mecca and before the revelation of the entire Surah?

Furthermore, regarding the last verse—namely His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ** (O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion...) [Qarai 5:54]—is it connected to the three verses preceding it? And who is intended by "these people" whose apostasy is anticipated?

And who are "these others" whom Allah promised He would bring? Each of these matters adds ambiguity upon ambiguity. The reports concerning the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) are scattered and represent nothing more than the views of the early exegetes, as is the case with most transmitted reports of revelation regarding the verses. This gross discrepancy also confuses the mind in understanding the meaning. Add to all this the interference of sectarian biases in the judgments passed on them, as you will encounter evidence of this in the narrations and the statements of exegetes from both the early and later generations.

What contemplation (التدبر) upon the verses yields is that these four verses, as we have cited them, are connected in their parts but detached from what precedes and follows them. The fourth verse is among the completions of the purpose intended for clarification within them. However, one must exercise caution in understanding their meaning, avoiding the laxity and oversimplifications permitted by the views of researching exegetes regarding the verses, especially concerning the attributes and descriptions mentioned therein, as will follow.

The summary of what is obtained from the verses is that Allah, the Glorified, warns the believers therein against taking the Jews and Christians as guardians (أولياء), threatening them most severely in that regard. He points out, in a Quranic prophecy (ملحمة قرآنية), what this alliance will lead to in terms of the collapse of the structure of the religious way of life, and that Allah will raise a people who will take charge of the matter and restore the structure of the religion to its original flourishing state.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ** (O you who have faith! Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies: they are allies of each other...) [Qarai 5:51]. It is stated in *al-Majma'*: "**Taking** (الاتخاذ) is the reliance upon a thing to prepare it for a matter. It is the *ifti'al* form from 'taking' (الأخذ). Its origin is *al-i'tikhadh*, where the *hamzah* was changed to a *ta'* and elided into the following *ta'*. Similar to it is *al-itti'ad* from 'promise' (الوعد). 'Taking' (الأخذ) occurs in several ways: you say 'he took the book' when he received it, 'he took the offering' when he accepted it, and 'Allah took him from his place of safety' when He destroyed him. Its root is the passage of a thing from one direction to another." (End of quote).

Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "**Proximity** (الولاء) and **succession** (التوالي) occur when two or more things happen such that there is nothing between them that does not belong to them. This is used metaphorically for closeness in terms of place, lineage, religion, friendship, support, and belief." (End of the relevant portion). The full investigation into the meaning of guardianship (الولاية) will follow.

In short, guardianship (الولاية) is a type of proximity to a thing that necessitates the removal of barriers and veils between them from the perspective for which the proximity was sought. If it is from the perspective of protection and seeking aid, then the guardian (الولي) is the helper (الناصر) whom nothing prevents from aiding the one he has drawn close to. If it is from the perspective of harmony in social interaction and love—which is spiritual attraction—then the guardian is the beloved (المحبوب) whom a person cannot help but be influenced by his will, giving him what he desires. If it is from the perspective of lineage, then the guardian is the one who inherits from him, for example, without any barrier preventing him. If it is from the perspective of obedience, then the guardian is the one who rules over his affairs as he wishes.

Allah, the Glorified, did not restrict guardianship in His saying: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ** (Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies) with any specificities or conditions; thus, it is absolute (مطلقة). However, His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse: **فَتَرَى الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ يُسارِعُونَ فِيهِمْ يَقُولُونَ نَخْشى أَنْ تُصِيبَنا دائِرَةٌ** (Yet you see those in whose hearts is a sickness rushing to them, saying, ‘We fear lest a turn of fortune should strike us’...) [Qarai 5:52] indicates that what is intended by guardianship is a type of proximity and connection suitable for the excuse they offered by saying: **نَخْشى أَنْ تُصِيبَنا دائِرَةٌ** (We fear lest a turn of fortune should strike us).

The "turn of fortune" (الدائرة) is the state of affairs turning against them. Just as it is possible for the turn of fortune to strike them from other than the Jews and Christians—whereby they would be bolstered by the aid of the two groups by taking them as guardians of support (أولياء النصرة)—it is also possible for it to strike them from the Jews and Christians themselves, whereby they would escape it by taking them as guardians of love and social mixing (أولياء المحبة والخلطة).

Guardianship in the sense of the proximity of love and social mixing combines both benefits—namely, the benefit of support and spiritual integration. This is what is intended by the verse. What is contained in the conditions and attributes mentioned in the last verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ** (O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion...) [Qarai 5:54] will follow, providing evidence that the guardianship intended here is the guardianship of love and nothing else.

Some exegetes have insisted that the guardianship intended is the guardianship of support (ولاية النصرة), which occurs between two individuals or two peoples through an alliance (الحلف) or a covenant (العهد) to aid one of the two guardians when needed. They argued for this with what can be summarized as follows: the verses—as appears from their literal sense—were revealed before the Farewell Pilgrimage, in the early period of the Migration, during the days when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Muslims had not yet finished dealing with the Jews of Medina and those around them from the Jews of Fadak, Khaybar, and others, with the Christians behind them. There were contracts of guardianship of support and alliance between groups of Arabs and them. This perhaps corresponds to what has been reported in the occasions of revelation: that 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit from the Banu 'Awf ibn al-Khazraj disassociated himself from the Banu Qaynuqa' when they fought the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), though there had been an alliance of guardianship between him and them. However, 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the head of the hypocrites, did not disassociate from them and rushed to them, saying: **نَخْشى أَنْ تُصِيبَنا دائِرَةٌ** (We fear lest a turn of fortune should strike us).

Or what was reported in the story of Abu Lubabah when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent him to bring the Banu Qurayzah out of their fortress to submit to his judgment, and Abu Lubabah pointed with his hand to his throat, indicating slaughter.

Or what was reported that some of them used to write to the Christians of Syria with news of Medina, and some used to write to the Jews of Medina to benefit from their wealth, even if by loan.

Or what was reported that some of them said they would join such-and-such Jew or such-and-such Christian following the killing and defeat that befell them on the day of Uhud.

These narrations are almost in agreement that those who said: **نَخْشى أَنْ تُصِيبَنا دائِرَةٌ** (We fear lest a turn of fortune should strike us) were the hypocrites. In short, the verses only forbid the alliance and guardianship of support between the Muslims and the Jews and Christians.

Some have emphasized this to the point of claiming that the guardianship in the verse being in the sense of the guardianship of love and reliance is something that the language of the verse—in its vocabulary and context—disavows, just as the occasions of revelation and the general state of the Muslims and the People of the Book (الكتابيون) during the era of revelation disavow it.

How could it be correct to interpret the verse as a prohibition of social interaction and mixing with them, even if they were people of the covenant (ذوي ذمة) or treaty, when the Jews were residing with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the companions in Medina, and they were treated with complete equality? (End of the summary of what he mentioned).

All of this stems from laxity in deriving the meaning of the verse. As for what they mentioned regarding the verses being revealed before the year of the Farewell Pilgrimage—which is the year of the revelation of Surah al-Ma'idah—there is not much problem with that, but it does not necessitate that guardianship be in the sense of alliance rather than the guardianship of love.

As for what they mentioned regarding the occasions of revelation and their indication that the verses were revealed specifically regarding the alliance and guardianship of support that existed between groups of Arabs and the Jews and Christians, there are several points: (First) the occasions of revelation are themselves contradictory and do not return to a single meaning that can be trusted and relied upon. (Second) they do not explain the guardianship of the Christians, even if they explain the guardianship of the Jews in some way, as there was no alliance of guardianship between the Arab Muslims and the Christians at that time. (Third) we accept the occasions of revelation in what they necessitate, but you have already learned that most of the narrations regarding the occasions of revelation—despite their weakness—consist of applying historically reported events to the Quranic verses suitable for them, and there is no harm in this.

However, to judge that the events mentioned therein specify the generality (عموم) of a Quranic verse or restrict its absolute (إطلاق) sense according to the wording is something that should not be uttered, nor does the apparent understood meaning support it. If the apparent sense of the verses were specified or restricted by a particularity in the occasion of revelation not taken from the wording of the verse, the Quran would have died with the death of those regarding whom it was revealed, and its use as an argument in any event occurring after the era of revelation would have ceased. This is not supported by the Book, the Sunnah, or sound intellect.

As for what some mentioned: "that taking guardianship to mean love and reliance is an error that the language of the verse in its vocabulary and context disavows, just as the occasions of revelation and the general state of the Muslims and the People of the Book during the era of revelation disavow it"—this is something that yields no coherent meaning upon reflection. What he mentioned regarding the disavowal of the occasions of revelation and the general state is that the verses include that and apply to it if the apparent sense of the verse does not refuse to correspond to it. As for limiting the indication to the instance of revelation and the general state existing at that time, you have learned that there is no evidence for it; rather, the evidence—which is the authoritative nature of the verse in its absolute apparent sense—is to the contrary. You have learned that the verse is absolute without evidence for its restriction, so it is authoritative in its absolute meaning, which is guardianship in the sense of love.

What he mentioned regarding the verse's vocabulary and context disavowing that is a strange statement. I wonder what he intended by this "disavowal" which he described and attributed to the vocabulary of the verse, not being content with that until he coupled it with its context.

How can the vocabulary or context of the verse disavow that when there occurs within it, after His saying: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ** (Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies), His saying, the Exalted: **بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ** (they are allies of each other)? There is no doubt that what is intended by this guardianship is the guardianship of love, unity, and affection, rather than the guardianship of alliance. For it makes no sense to say: "Do not ally with the Jews and Christians, for they are allies of each other," when what creates unity among the Jews and refers some of them to others is the guardianship of nationalistic love, and similarly among the Christians, without any alliance or covenant between them except mere love and affection from the perspective of religion.

Similarly, His saying, the Exalted, after that: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ** (Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them) [Qarai 5:51]. The consideration that necessitates that the one who allies with a group becomes one of that group is that love and affection gather the scattered, unify different souls, and thereby unify perceptions, link character traits, and make actions similar. You see the two who love each other, after the guardianship of love is established, as if they are one person with one psyche, one will, and one action; neither of them disagrees with the other in the course of life and the level of social interaction.

This is what necessitates that whoever allies with a people becomes one of them and joins them. It has been said: "Whoever loves a people is of them," and "A man is with whom he loves." Allah, the Exalted, said in a similar vein, forbidding the alliance with the polytheists: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا بِما جاءَكُمْ مِنَ الْحَقِ** (O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, offering them affection, while they have defied the truth that has come to you...) until He said after several verses: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ** (and whoever allies with them—it is they who are the wrongdoers) [Qarai 60:1-9]. And the Exalted said: **لا تَجِدُ قَوْماً يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ يُوادُّونَ مَنْ حَادَّ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَلَوْ كانُوا آباءَهُمْ أَوْ أَبْناءَهُمْ أَوْ إِخْوانَهُمْ أَوْ عَشِيرَتَهُمْ** (You will not find a people believing in Allah and the Last Day love those who oppose Allah and His Apostle, even though they be their fathers, or their sons, or their brothers, or their clansmen...) [Qarai 58:22]. And the Exalted said regarding allying with the disbelievers—and the wording is general, including the Jews, Christians, and polytheists all together: **لا يَتَّخِذِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الْكافِرِينَ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ اللهِ فِي شَيْءٍ إِلَّا أَنْ تَتَّقُوا مِنْهُمْ تُقاةً وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ اللهُ نَفْسَهُ** (The faithful should not take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful, and Allah will have nothing to do with him who does that, except when you are wary of them out of caution. Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him...) [Qarai 3:28]. This verse is explicit regarding the guardianship of affection and love, rather than alliance and covenant. There were treaties and peace agreements between the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Jews, as well as between him and the polytheists at that time—meaning the time of the revelation of Surah Al 'Imran.

In short, the guardianship that necessitates, according to rational consideration, the joining of one people to another is the guardianship of love and affection, not alliance and support, and this is apparent. If the meaning of His saying: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ** (Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them) were that whoever allies with them for support after this prohibition is, due to his disobedience of the prohibition, a wrongdoer joined to those wrongdoers in wrongdoing, it would be a meaning—despite its banality—far from the wording, requiring additional conditions in the speech.

It is the custom of the Quran, in everything it forbids that was permissible and allowed before the prohibition, to point to it out of regard for the previously legislated ruling and respect for the Prophetic practice (السيرة النبوية) that preceded it, such as His saying: **إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ بَعْدَ عامِهِمْ هذا** (Indeed the polytheists are impure. So let them not approach the Holy Mosque after this year of theirs...) [Qarai 9:28], and His saying: **فَالْآنَ بَاشِرُوهُنَّ وَابْتَغُوا ما كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ وَكُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا** (...So now associate with them, and seek what Allah has ordained for you, and eat and drink...) [Qarai 2:187], and His saying: **لا يَحِلُّ لَكَ النِّساءُ مِنْ بَعْدُ وَلا أَنْ تَبَدَّلَ بِهِنَّ مِنْ أَزْواجٍ** (Beyond that, women are not lawful for you, nor that you should replace them with other wives...) [Qarai 33:52], among others.

Thus, it has become clear that the language of the verse in its vocabulary and context does not disavow the guardianship intended being the guardianship of love and affection; rather, if it disavows anything, it disavows other than that.

As for their statement that those intended by "those in whose hearts is a sickness" are the hypocrites, it will follow that the context does not support it. Therefore, the meaning of His saying: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ** (Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies) is the prohibition of affection for them, which necessitates the mutual attraction of souls and spirits that leads to moral influence and being influenced. For that would transform the state of their society from the religious way of life based on the felicity of following the truth to the way of life of disbelief based on following whims, worshipping Satan, and exiting the path of primordial nature (الفطرة).

He referred to them as "the Jews and the Christians" and did not refer to them as "the People of the Book" as He did in the subsequent verse, because the expression "People of the Book" suggests a type of closeness to the Muslims that might stir affection, which is unsuitable for the prohibition of taking them as guardians. As for what is in the subsequent verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَكُمْ هُزُواً وَلَعِباً مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَالْكُفَّارَ أَوْلِياءَ** (O you who have faith! Do not take those who take your religion in mockery and play, from among those who were given the Book before you, and the faithless, for allies...) [Qarai 5:57], describing them as having been given the Book alongside the prohibition of taking them as guardians, their description as taking the religion of Allah in mockery and play transforms that attribute—namely, their being people of the Book—from praise to blame. For one who was given the Book that calls to the truth and clarifies it, and then proceeds to mock the religion of truth and play with it, is most worthy and deserving of not being taken as a guardian, and his social interaction, mixing, and affection should be avoided.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ** (they are allies of each other), the intended guardianship—as previously mentioned—is the guardianship of love that entails the proximity of their souls and the mutual attraction of their spirits, which necessitates the gathering of their views upon following whims, arrogance against the truth and its acceptance, unity in extinguishing the light of Allah, the Glorified, and mutual support against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Muslims as if they were a single soul with a single creed. They are not united in creed, but what drives the groups to agreement and makes them a single hand against the Muslims is that Islam calls them to the truth and opposes their dearest objectives, which are following whims and indulging in the desires of the soul and the pleasures of the world.

This is what made the two groups—the Jews and the Christians—despite the discord and severe enmity between them, a single community where some draw close to others and some return to others, the Jews allying with the Christians and vice versa, and some Jews allying with other Jews, and some Christians with other Christians. This is the meaning of the ambiguity of the sentence: **بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ** (they are allies of each other) in its vocabulary. The sentence is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for His saying: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ** (Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies). The meaning is: do not take them as guardians because, despite their division and discord among themselves, they are a single hand against you; there is no benefit for you in drawing close to them through affection and love.

It might be possible to derive another meaning from His saying: **بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ**, which is: do not take them as guardians because you only take them as guardians to be aided by some of them—who are your guardians—against others, but that will not benefit you, for they are guardians of each other and will not aid you against themselves.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** (Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them. Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people) [Qarai 5:51]. **Allying** (التولي) is the taking of a guardian, and "of" (من) is partitive (تبعيضية). The meaning is that whoever among you takes them as guardians is part of them. This is a figurative joining (إلحاق تنزيلي) by which some believers become part of the Jews and Christians. The matter boils down to faith being a reality with different levels in terms of admixture and purity, turbidity and clarity, as is derived from the Quranic verses. The Exalted said: **وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ** (And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him) [Qarai 12:106]. This admixture and turbidity is what the Exalted expresses as "sickness of the hearts" in what follows of His saying: **فَتَرَى الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ يُسارِعُونَ فِيهِمْ** (Yet you see those in whose hearts is a sickness rushing to them...).

These who ally with those are groups whom Allah has counted among the Jews and Christians, even if they are outwardly among the believers. The least of it is that they are not treading the path of guidance (الهدى) which is faith; rather, they are treading a path that those have taken as a path, which leads them where it leads those and ends with them where it ends with those.

Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, reasoned his joining them by His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** (Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people). The speech is in the sense that: this one among you who allies with them is of them, not treading your path, because the path of faith is the path of divine guidance, and this one who allies with them is a wrongdoer like them, and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people.

The verse—as you see—contains the principle of the figurative joining—namely, joining the one among the believers who allies with them to their status—without addressing any of its subsidiary effects. Although the wording is not restricted by a condition, since it is of the nature of clarifying the underlying rationale (الملاك)—similar to His saying: **وَأَنْ تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ** (...and that you fast is better for you...) [Qarai 2:184] and His saying: **إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ تَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ** (...Indeed prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs, and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater...) [Qarai 29:45], among others—it is merely an underspecified statement (مهمل) that requires the Sunnah to be invoked for establishing a subsidiary ruling, and the reference for investigating that is the science of jurisprudence (الفقه).

His saying, the Exalted: **فَتَرَى الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ يُسارِعُونَ فِيهِمْ** (Yet you see those in whose hearts is a sickness rushing to them...) [Qarai 5:52] is a derivation from His saying in the previous verse: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** (Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people). From the lack of divine guidance encompassing their state—which is misguidance—is their rushing to them and their offering of excuses that are not to be heard. The Exalted said: **يُسارِعُونَ فِيهِمْ** (rushing *in* them) and did not say "rushing *to* them," for they are of them and reside in misguidance in their place. These people rush in them not out of fear of a turn of fortune striking them, for they do not fear that; rather, it is an excuse they fabricate for themselves to repel the blame and reproach directed at them from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers. Instead, what drives them to that rushing is their allying with those (the Jews and Christians).

Since it is the nature of every wrongdoing and falsehood to perish one day and for its scandal to appear to the public, and for the hope of whoever sought false goals through means whose form is the form of truth to be cut off—as the Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ**—it was certainly to be expected that Allah would bring a victory or a command from Him, so they would regret their actions, and their lie in what they were manifesting would appear to the believers.

By this explanation, the reason for the derivation of His saying: **فَتَرَى الَّذِينَ** (Yet you see those...) from His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** becomes clear. Discussion regarding the lack of guidance for the wrongdoers in their wrongdoing has preceded.

These people are hypocrites from the perspective of manifesting to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers what is not in their hearts, as they label their rushing to the Jews and Christians with the label of fear of a turn of fortune, while its true label, corresponding to what is in their hearts, is allying with the enemies of Allah. This is the aspect of their hypocrisy. As for their being hypocrites in the sense of disbelievers who manifest faith, the context of the verses does not support it.

A group of exegetes mentioned that they are the hypocrites like 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy and his companions, as supported by the reported occasions of revelation. For these hypocrites used to participate with the believers in their society and flatter them on one hand, while on the other hand, they used to ally with the Jews and Christians through alliance and covenant for support, seeking to draw benefit from both groups and taking precaution in looking after their own interests so they would be favored in any case, and be safe from a turn of fortune regardless of which of the two contending groups it turned against.

What they mentioned does not suit the context of the verses, for they contain the expectation that they will regret by a victory or a command from Him. The victory is the Conquest of Mecca, or the conquest of the fortresses of the Jews and the lands of the Christians, or the like, as they said. There is no reason for their regret on this assumption, for they took precaution in their affair by preserving both sides, and there is no regret in precaution. Regret would only be correct if they had cut themselves off from the believers entirely and connected with the Jews and Christians, and then the turn of fortune went against them. Similarly, what Allah, the Exalted, mentioned regarding the nullification of their deeds and their becoming losers by His saying: **حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا خاسرِينَ** (...their works have failed, and they have become losers) [Qarai 5:53] does not suit their being the hypocrites who take precaution for their benefits and goals. For there is no meaning to the loss of one who took precaution as a safeguard against a disliked thing he feared for himself, and then it happened that what he feared did not occur. Precaution in action is among the rational methods that do not entail blame or censure.

Unless it is said: the blame only attached to them because they disobeyed the divine prohibition and their hearts were not at peace with what Allah promised of victory. This, although not problematic in itself, has no evidence from the wording of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَعَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِالْفَتْحِ أَوْ أَمْرٍ مِنْ عِنْدِهِ فَيُصْبِحُوا عَلى ما أَسَرُّوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ نادِمِينَ** (Maybe Allah will bring about a victory, or a command from Himself, and then they will be regretful for what they kept secret in their hearts) [Qarai 5:52]. The word "maybe" (عسى), although in His speech, the Exalted, it is for expectation (الترجي) like other speech—as we have previously stated that it is for expectation returning to the listener or to the context—the context provides evidence that it is something that will certainly occur. For the speech is aimed at affirming what He mentioned by His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** and establishing its truth; thus, what it contains is inevitably real.

What Allah, the Exalted, mentioned of "the victory" (الفتح)—having alternated between it and "a command from Himself" without clarifying the specific instance, rather alternating between it and a command unknown to us—perhaps supports that the *lam* in **بِالْفَتْحِ** is for the genus (للجنس) and not for the specific (للعهد), such that it would mean the Conquest of Mecca, which was known by the promise of its occurrence in verses like His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرادُّكَ إِلى مَعادٍ** (Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the destination...) [Qarai 28:85] and His saying: **لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ** (...You will surely enter the Holy Mosque, if Allah wills...) [Qarai 48:27], and others.

The "victory" (الفتح) occurring in the Quran, although in most of its instances it refers to the Conquest of Mecca, some instances do not accept being interpreted as such, like His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْفَتْحُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ قُلْ يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ لا يَنْفَعُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِيمانُهُمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَانْتَظِرْ إِنَّهُمْ مُنْتَظِرُونَ** (They say, ‘When will this victory be, if you are truthful?’ Say, ‘On the day of victory their faith will not benefit the faithless, nor will they be granted any respite.’ So turn away from them and wait; they too are waiting) [Qarai 32:28-30]. For the Exalted described this victory as being such that faith will not benefit one who was a disbeliever before it, and that the disbelievers are waiting for it. You know that this does not apply to the Conquest of Mecca nor to the other victories achieved by the Muslims until today. For the lack of benefit of faith—which is repentance (التوبة)—is only conceivable for one of two reasons, as previously explained in the discussion on repentance: either by the transformation of the state of life and the removal of choice due to the world being replaced by the Hereafter, or by the formation of character traits and dispositions in the human being by which the heart hardens with a hardness that leaves no hope for repentance and return to Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُآياتِ رَبِّكَ لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها لَمْ تَكُنْ آمَنَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً** (...The day when some of your Lord’s signs do come, faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed beforehand and had not earned some good through its faith...) [Qarai 6:158]. And the Exalted said: **وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ وَلَا الَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ** (But repentance is not for those who go on committing sins until, when death visits one of them, he says, ‘I repent now!’—nor for those who die while they are faithless...) [Qarai 4:18].

In any case, if the intended victory is one of the victories of the Muslims, such as the Conquest of Mecca, the conquest of the Jewish fortresses, or the conquest of the Christian lands, then so be it. However, there is an obscurity in the correspondence of what Allah, the Exalted, mentioned by His saying: **فَيُصْبِحُوا عَلى ما أَسَرُّوا** (and then they will be regretful for what they kept secret...) and His saying: **وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ** (and the faithful will say...), the reason for which has been mentioned.

If the intended victory is victory in the sense of the judgment for Islam over disbelief and the decisive ruling between the Messenger and his people, then it is among the Quranic prophecies in which the Exalted informs of the events that will face this nation in the future. This corresponds to what Allah mentioned in Surah Yunus: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ** (There is an apostle for every nation; so when their apostle comes, judgment is passed between them...) until the end of the verses [Qarai 10:47-56].

As for His saying: **فَيُصْبِحُوا عَلى ما أَسَرُّوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ نادِمِينَ** (and then they will be regretful for what they kept secret in their hearts), regret (الندامة) only occurs upon doing what should not have been done or leaving what should not have been left. They have done something, and Allah, the Glorified, mentions in the following verse the nullification of their deeds and their loss in their transaction. For they only kept secret in their hearts the allying with the Jews and Christians to attain thereby, and by rushing to them, what the Jews and Christians wanted of extinguishing the light of Allah and dominating over worldly desires without any hindrance from religion.

This—perhaps—is what they kept secret in their hearts, and for which they rushed to them, and they will regret the futility of their effort when Allah opens the way for the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (and the faithful will say...) [Qarai 5:53] until the end of the verse. It has been recited as **يَقُولُ** with the accusative case (النصب) as a conjunction to His saying: **فَيُصْبِحُوا**, and this is more likely as it is more in accord with the context. For their regret over what they kept secret in their hearts and the saying of the believers: **أَهؤُلاءِ** (Are these...?) are both a reproach to them for the consequence of their allying and rushing to the Jews and Christians. His saying: **هؤُلاءِ** (these) is an indication toward the Jews and Christians, and His saying: **لَمَعَكُمْ** (with you) is an address to those in whose hearts is a sickness. The reverse is also possible. Similarly, the pronoun in His saying: **حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا** (their works have failed, and they have become...) can return to the Jews and Christians, or to those in whose hearts is a sickness.

However, it is apparent from the context that the address is to those in whose hearts is a sickness, the indication is to the Jews and Christians, and His saying: **حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ** (their works have failed) is like an answer to a presumed question. The meaning is: maybe Allah will bring about a victory or a command from Himself, and then the believers will say to these of weak faith, when the divine wrath descends upon them: "Are these Jews and Christians the ones who swore by Allah with their most solemn oaths—that is, the oaths they emphasized and exerted effort in—that they were with you? Why then do they not benefit you?!" Then, as if it were asked: "What was the end of the affair of these allies?" it was said in response: "Their works have failed, and they have become losers."

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of the Sickness of the Heart)

In His saying, the Exalted: **فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ** (in whose hearts is a sickness), there is an indication that hearts have a sickness, so they must necessarily have health (صحة), as health and sickness are opposites; neither is realized in a locus except after the possibility of its being characterized by the other, like sight and blindness. Do you not see that a wall, for example, is not described as being sick because it cannot be described as having health and soundness?

In all the instances where Allah, the Glorified, establishes a sickness for the hearts in His speech, He mentions among the states of those hearts and their effects matters that indicate their departure from the uprightness of primordial nature (الفطرة) and their deviation from the level path, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ ما وَعَدَنَا اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً** (And when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness said, ‘Allah and His Apostle did not promise us [anything] but delusion’) [Qarai 33:12], and His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ** (When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness said, ‘Their religion has deluded them’...) [Qarai 8:49], and His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَجْعَلَ ما يُلْقِي الشَّيْطانُ فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ** (...that He may make what Satan casts a temptation for those in whose hearts is a sickness and whose hearts are hard...) [Qarai 22:53], among others.

The sum of the matter is that the sickness of the heart (مرض القلب) is its being characterized by a type of doubt (الارتياب) and uncertainty (الشك) that turbidifies the matter of faith in Allah and tranquility in His signs. It is an admixture of faith with polytheism (الشرك). Therefore, there occur to such a heart states, and there issue from the possessor of such a heart in the stage of deeds and actions, things that suit disbelief in Allah and His signs.

By contrast, the soundness (سلامة) of the heart and its health is its stability in the uprightness of primordial nature and its adherence to the level path. It boils down to its sincerity in the monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — of Allah, the Glorified, and its reliance upon Him away from everything to which human whim attaches. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ** (the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart) [Qarai 26:88-89].

From here it appears that those in whose hearts is a sickness are other than the hypocrites (المنافقون), just as the Quranic expression regarding them both—such as His saying: **الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ** (the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness)—in most instances is not devoid of some indication of that. This is because the hypocrites are those who believed with their mouths while their hearts did not believe, and specific disbelief is a death (موت) for the heart, not a sickness in it. The Exalted said: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأحيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** (Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people...) [Qarai 6:122], and He said: **إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ وَالْمَوْتى يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ** (Only those who listen will respond; as for the dead, Allah will resurrect them...) [Qarai 6:36].

Thus, it appears that the sickness of the heart in the parlance of the Quran is the doubt and uncertainty that overcomes the human perception regarding what pertains to Allah and His signs, and the heart's inability to commit to a religious creed.

So, those in whose hearts is a sickness, according to the nature of the meaning, are those of weak faith who listen to every caller and incline with every wind, unlike the hypocrites who manifested faith and concealed disbelief out of regard for their worldly interests, so they could draw benefit from the believers by their outward faith and from the disbelievers by their inward disbelief.

Yes, perhaps they are called hypocrites in the Quran by way of analysis, because they share with them the fact that their interior does not contain the subtlety of faith. This is different from calling those who are disbelievers and only believed outwardly "those in whose hearts is a sickness." The Exalted said: **بَشِّرِ الْمُنافِقِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً الَّذِينَ يَتَّخِذُونَ الْكافِرِينَ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ أَنْ إِذا سَمِعْتُمْ آياتِ اللهِ يُكْفَرُ بِها وَيُسْتَهْزَأُ بِها فَلا تَقْعُدُوا مَعَهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ جامِعُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَالْكافِرِينَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ جَمِيعاً** (Inform the hypocrites that there is a painful punishment for them—those who take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful. Do they seek honour with them? Indeed all honour belongs to Allah. He has already revealed to you in the Book that when you hear Allah’s signs being denied and mocked, do not sit with them until they engage in some other talk, or else you [too] will be like them. Indeed Allah will gather the hypocrites and the faithless in hell all together) [Qarai 4:138-140].

As for His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Baqarah: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَبِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَما هُمْ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ** (Among the people are those who say, ‘We believe in Allah and the Last Day,’ but they do not believe) until He said: **فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَهُمُ اللهُ مَرَضاً** (In their hearts is a sickness, and Allah has increased their sickness...) until He said: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ آمِنُوا كَما آمَنَ النَّاسُ قالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ كَما آمَنَ السُّفَهاءُ** (And when they are told, ‘Believe like the people have believed,’ they say, ‘Shall we believe like the fools have believed?’...) [Qarai 2:8-13]. This is merely a clarification of the course of their hearts from doubt in the truth to its denial, and that they were in the beginning of their state sick because of their lie in reporting their faith, and they were doubters who had not yet believed. So Allah increased them in sickness until they perished by their denial of the truth and their mocking of it.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned that the sickness of the heart, like physical illnesses, may increase until it becomes chronic and leads to destruction, and that is by supplying it with what harms the nature of the sick person in his sickness, which is nothing but sin (المعصية). The Exalted said: **فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَهُمُ اللهُ مَرَضاً** (In their hearts is a sickness, and Allah has increased their sickness...) [Qarai 2:10]. And the Exalted said: **وَإِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ** (Whenever a surah is sent down...) until He said: **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَتْهُمْ رِجْساً إِلَى رِجْسِهِمْ وَماتُوا وَهُمْ كافِرُونَ أَوَلا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِي كُلِّ عامٍ مَرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ لا يَتُوبُونَ وَلا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ** (...But as for those in whose hearts is a sickness, it only adds profanity to their profanity, and they die while they are faithless. Do they not see that they are tested once or twice every year? Yet they neither repent, nor do they take admonition) [Qarai 9:124-126]. And the Exalted said—which is a general clarification: **ثُمَّ كانَ عاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا السُّواى أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ وَكانُوا بِها يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ** (Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah and they used to mock them) [Qarai 30:10].

Then the Exalted mentioned faith in Him as its cure. The Exalted said—which is a general clarification: **يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِإِيمانِهِمْ** (...their Lord guides them because of their faith...) [Qarai 10:9]. And the Exalted said: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ** (...To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous deeds...) [Qarai 35:10]. So the one with a sick heart—if he wishes to treat his sickness—must repent to Allah, which is faith in Him, and he must take admonition through righteous thought and righteous deed, as indicated by the previously mentioned verse: **ثُمَّ لا يَتُوبُونَ وَلا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ** (...Yet they neither repent, nor do they take admonition) [Qarai 9:126].

And the Glorified said—which is a comprehensive statement in this chapter: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْكافِرِينَ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَتُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَجْعَلُوا لِلَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطاناً مُبِيناً إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُمْ نَصِيراً إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِاللهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَسَوْفَ يُؤْتِ اللهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً** (O you who have faith! Do not take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful. Do you wish to give Allah a clear sanction against yourselves? Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire, and you will never find any helper for them, except those who repent and reform, and hold fast to Allah and dedicate their religion exclusively to Allah. Those are with the faithful, and soon Allah will give the faithful a great reward) [Qarai 4:144-146]. It has preceded that what is intended by that is the return to Allah through faith, steadfastness upon it, and adhering to the Book and the Sunnah, then sincerity (الإخلاص).

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ** (O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion...) [Qarai 5:54]. "Deserted his religion" (ارتد عن دينه) means he returned from it. In the terminology of the people of religion, it is the return from faith to disbelief, whether his faith was preceded by another disbelief—like the disbeliever who believes and then apostatizes—or not. These are the two named national apostasy (الارتداد الملي) and primordial apostasy (الارتداد الفطري) — *a legal or religious reality*.

It might occur to the mind that the intended apostasy (الارتداد) in the verse is what the people of religion have termed, and thus the verse would not be connected to what precedes it, but would be an independent verse recounting a type of Allah's, the Glorified, lack of need for the faith of a group of believers through the faith of others.

However, contemplation upon the verse and the verses preceding it repels this possibility. For the verse, on this basis, would be reminding the believers of the power of Allah, the Glorified, to be worshipped in His earth, and that He will bring people who do not apostatize from His religion but rather adhere to it, like His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ يَكْفُرْ بِها هؤُلاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنا بِها قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ** (...So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will not disbelieve in them) [Qarai 6:89], or like His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ** (...and should anyone disbelieve, Allah is indeed independent of all the worlds) [Qarai 3:97], and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ تَكْفُرُوا أَنْتُمْ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً فَإِنَّ اللهَ لَغَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ** (...‘If you and all those who are on the earth disbelieve, Allah is indeed all-sufficient, all-laudable’) [Qarai 14:8].

A context of this description does not necessitate more than addressing the primary purpose, which is informing of the bringing of a believing people who do not apostatize from the religion of Allah. As for their loving Allah and Him loving them, and their being lowly toward the believers and mighty against the disbelievers, until the end of the attributes mentioned in the verse—these are additional matters whose mention requires an additional necessity from the context and state.

From another perspective, we find that the attributes mentioned in the verse are matters not devoid of connection to what was mentioned in the previous verses regarding allying with the Jews and Christians. For taking them as guardians instead of the believers is not devoid of the heart's attachment to them with the attachment of love and affection. How can a heart of this state contain the love of Allah, the Glorified, when the Exalted has said: **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِنْ قَلْبَيْنِ فِي جَوْفِهِ** (Allah has not put two hearts within any man’s breast...) [Qarai 33:4]? Among the requirements of this alliance is that the believer humbles himself to these disbelievers, and that he acts mightily against the believers and elevates himself above them, as the Exalted said: **أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** (Do they seek honour with them? Indeed all honour belongs to Allah) [Qarai 4:139].

Among the requirements of this alliance is laxity in jihad against them, reluctance to fight them, and the difficulty of enduring every deprivation and bearing every blame in cutting social ties with them, as the Exalted said: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا بِما جاءَكُمْ مِنَ الْحَقِ** (O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, offering them affection, while they have defied the truth that has come to you...) until He said: **إِنْ كُنْتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهاداً فِي سَبِيلِي وَابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِي تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ** (...if you have set out for jihad in My way and to seek My pleasure, secretly proffering them affection...) [Qarai 60:1]. And the Exalted said: **قَدْ كانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ إِذْ قالُوا لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَآؤُا مِنْكُمْ وَمِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ كَفَرْنا بِكُمْ وَبَدا بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةُ وَالْبَغْضاءُ أَبَداً حَتَّى تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَحْدَهُ** (There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham and those who were with him, when they said to their own people, ‘Indeed we repudiate you and whatever you worship besides Allah. We deny you, and enmity and hatred have appeared between us and you for ever, until you believe in Allah alone’...) [Qarai 60:4].

Similarly, apostasy (الارتداد) in its linguistic sense or through analytical consideration applies to allying with the disbelievers, as the Exalted said in the previous verse (verse 51): **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ** (Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them), and He also said: **وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ اللهِ فِي شَيْءٍ** (...and Allah will have nothing to do with him who does that...) [Qarai 3:28], and the Exalted said: **إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ** (...or else you [too] will be like them...) [Qarai 4:140].

It has become clear by this explanation that the verse has a connection to the verses preceding it, and that the verse is aimed at showing that the religion of Allah is independent of those for whom there is fear of falling into the pit of opposition and allying with the Jews and Christians due to the creeping of hypocrisy into their group, and its inclusion of a number of those with sick hearts who do not care about purchasing the world for the religion, and preferring the false honor and vanishing vital status found with the enemies of religion over the true honor which belongs to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers, and the real felicity encompassing the life of this world and the Hereafter.

The verse only showed that by informing of a prophecy of the Unseen (ملحمة غيبية) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — that Allah, the Glorified, in contrast to what the religion meets of the fickleness of these of weak faith, and their choosing the love of other than Allah over His love, and seeking honor with His enemies, and their laxity in jihad in His way, and fear of every blame and reproach, will bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, lowly toward the believers, mighty against the disbelievers, striving in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer.

Many exegetes, although they noticed the verse's inclusion of the prophecy and lengthily investigated whom the verse applies to as a specific instance (مصداق), nevertheless were lax in interpreting its vocabulary, so they did not give the attributes mentioned therein the right of their meaning. The matter ended with them treating the speech of Allah, the Glorified, like the speech of others, and permitting the occurrence of conventional oversimplifications and laxities in it as in others.

The Quran, although it did not follow an unprecedented path in its eloquence, nor did it adopt a newly invented way in the use of words, the composition of sentences, and the placement of words against their meanings, rather it followed in that the course of other speech.

However, it differs from all other speech in another respect: we, the community of speakers—whether eloquent or otherwise—construct our speech upon the foundation of the meanings we conceptualize. The meanings we perceive are grasped through an understanding acquired from the social life we have fashioned by our social human nature (الفطرة). It is the nature of this life to judge by analogy (القياس), and at that point, the door of indulgence (المسامحة) and laxity (المساهلة) opens to our minds. Thus, we take the "many" in place of the "all," the "predominant" in place of the "permanent"; we assume every analogical matter to be absolute, treat every rare occurrence as non-existent, and regard every trivial matter as if it does not exist.

One of us might say: "This is beautiful" or "ugly," "this is beloved" or "hated," "this is praised" or "blamed," "this is beneficial" or "harmful," or "so-and-so is good" or "evil," and so forth. We utter these as absolute statements, yet the reality is only such in certain states, under certain conditions, for certain people, and in comparison to certain things—not absolutely. However, the speaker treats some contrary conditions as non-existent out of indulgence in his perception and judgment. This pertains to what he perceives of the aspects of external reality; as for what he is heedless of due to the limitation of his perception regarding the interconnected aspects of the cosmos, that is even greater. Therefore, what a human being reports and relates about the external world, while imagining he has encompassed reality through perception and discovery, is actually built upon indulgence in some aspects and ignorance in others. It would be considered jest (الهزل) were we able to encompass reality and then apply his speech to it. Understand this well.

This is the state of human speech, built upon the knowledge they possess. As for the speech of Allah, the Glorified, it is mandatory that we exalt Him above this deficiency, for He is the One Who encompasses all things in knowledge. He, the Exalted, has said regarding the description of His speech: **إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ وَما هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ** "It is indeed a decisive word, and it is not a jest." (86:13-14).

This is one of the reasons for taking the absolute (إطلاق) of His speech, the Exalted, wherever it is apparently absolute and not followed by a connected or disconnected restriction. It is also a reason for the description in His speech to imply causality (العلية). Thus, when He says: **يُحِبُّهُمْ** "He loves them," He does not hate them in any respect, otherwise He would have made an exception. And when He describes a people as being lowly toward the believers, it is mandatory that they be lowly toward them by virtue of their being believers—that is, due to the attribute of their faith in Allah, the Glorified—and that they be lowly in all their states and under all conditions; otherwise, the word would not be "decisive" (فصل).

Yes, there are meanings attributed to those other than their primary subjects when there is a common factor that justifies such attribution, as in His saying: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ** "Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood, and We provided them with all the good things, and We gave them an advantage over all the nations." (45:16); and His saying: **هُوَ اجْتَباكُمْ وَما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ** "He has chosen you and has not placed for you any obstacle in religion." (22:78); and His saying: **كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ تَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَتَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** "You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind: you bid what is right and forbid what is wrong." (3:110); and His saying: **لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيداً** "so that you may be witnesses to mankind, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you." (2:143); and His saying: **وَقالَ الرَّسُولُ يا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُوراً** "And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion.’" (25:30), among other verses containing social descriptions (أوصاف اجتماعية) by which both the individual and the society are characterized. None of this follows the course of indulgence or laxity; rather, these are attributes by which the part and the whole, the individual and the society, are characterized due to the specific concern (عناية) of the related object. This is like a handful of soil containing a jewel; one grasps it for the sake of the jewel. Thus, the soil is grasped and the jewel is grasped, but the primary object is the jewel.

Let us return to our subject: As for His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ** "O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion," the meaning of apostasy (الارتداد) and turning back from the religion—based on what has preceded—is the taking of Jews and Christians as allies (موالاة). The address is specifically directed to the believers because the preceding address was also directed to them. The context is one of explaining that the True Religion is in no need of their faith which is tainted by the alliance with the enemies of Allah. Allah, the Glorified, has counted this as disbelief (كفر) and polytheism (شرك) where He said: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ** "Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them." (5:51), because Allah, the Glorified, is the Guardian (ولي) of His religion and its Helper. Part of His helping His religion is that He will bring a people who are free from His enemies, who take His friends as allies, and who love none but Him.

As for His saying: **فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ** "Allah will soon bring a people," He attributed the "bringing" to Himself to establish the meaning of His helping His religion, which is understood from the context. This context indicates that this religion has a Helper Who does not need the help of anyone else, and He is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه).

The fact that the speech is directed toward explaining the victory of the religion through these people—in contrast to the nationalistic victory sought by those who ally with His enemies—as well as the use of the term "people" (قوم) and the use of attributes and verbs in the plural form, all indicate that the promised people will be sent as a collective group (جماعة مجتمعين), not as individuals or pairs. It is not merely that Allah, the Glorified, brings in every age a single man who loves Allah and whom Allah loves, who is lowly toward the believers and mighty against the disbelievers, striving in the way of Allah and not fearing the blame of any blamer.

The bringing of these people, while attributed to them, is also attributed to Him, the Exalted; He is the One Who brings them. This is not in the sense that He is their Creator—for there is no creator but Allah, the Glorified, as He said: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62)—but rather in the sense that He is the One Who rouses them (الباعث لهم) toward the victory of the religion they undertake. He is the One Who honors them with His love for them and their love for Him, and Who grants them the success (التوفيق) to be lowly toward His friends, mighty against His enemies, to strive in His way, and to disregard all blame. Thus, their victory for the religion is His victory for it through them and by way of them. Near and far time are the same to Allah, even if our limited perspectives differentiate between them.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ** "He loves them and they love Him," love (الحب) here is absolute, attached to the essence (الذات) without being restricted by an attribute or otherwise. As for their love for Allah, its necessary consequence is their preferring their Lord over everything else besides Him to which the human soul is attached, such as wealth, status, kin, or other things. These people do not ally with any of the enemies of Allah, the Glorified; and if they ally with anyone, they only ally with the friends of Allah through the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, the Exalted.

As for His love for them, its necessary consequence is their innocence from every injustice (ظلم) and their purity from every spiritual filth (قذارة معنوية), such as disbelief and transgression, through divine protection (عصمة) or divine forgiveness (مغفرة) resulting from repentance. This is because the sum of injustices and sins are not loved by Allah, as He, the Exalted, said: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْكافِرِينَ** "for Allah does not love the faithless" (3:32); and He said: **وَاللهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ** "and Allah does not love the wrongdoers" (3:57); and He said: **إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ** "Indeed He does not love the prodigal" (6:141); and He said: **وَاللهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُفْسِدِينَ** "and Allah does not love the agents of corruption" (5:64); and He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ** "Indeed Allah does not love the transgressors" (2:190); and He said: **إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْتَكْبِرِينَ** "Indeed He does not love the arrogant" (16:23); and He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْخائِنِينَ** "Indeed Allah does not love the treacherous" (8:58), among other verses.

In these verses is the sum of human vices (الرذائل الإنسانية). If these are removed from a person by the testimony of Allah’s love for him, he becomes characterized by their opposite virtues (الفضائل), because a human being has no escape from one of the two sides—virtue or vice—once he adopts a character trait.

These are the true believers in Allah, whose faith is not tainted by injustice. Allah, the Exalted, has said: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided." (6:82). They are safe from straying (الضلال), and He, the Exalted, has said: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُّ** "for Allah does not guide those who mislead" (16:37). Thus, they are in divine safety from every deviation and upon divine guidance to His straight path (الصراط المستقيم). By their faith, which Allah has confirmed, they are guided to follow the Messenger and to submit completely to him, just as they submit to Allah, the Glorified. He, the Exalted, said: **فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً** "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their hearts any dissent to your verdict and submit in total submission." (4:65).

At that point, it becomes complete that they are among the instances (مصاديق) of His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللهُ** "Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you’" (3:31). From this, it appears that following the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the love of Allah are inseparable; whoever follows the Prophet, Allah loves him, and Allah does not love a servant unless he is a follower of His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

When they follow the Messenger, they become characterized by every good deed (حسنة) that Allah loves and is pleased with, such as godwariness (التقوى), justice (العدل), excellence (الإحسان), patience (الصبر), steadfastness (الثبات), trust (التوكل), repentance (التوبة), and purification (التطهر), among others. He, the Exalted, said: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ** "for Allah loves the God-conscious" (3:76); and He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** "Indeed Allah loves the virtuous" (2:195); and He said: **وَاللهُ يُحِبُّ الصَّابِرِينَ** "and Allah loves the steadfast" (3:146); and He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الَّذِينَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِهِ صَفًّا كَأَنَّهُمْ بُنْيانٌ مَرْصُوصٌ** "Indeed Allah loves those who fight in His way in ranks, as if they were a compact structure" (61:4); and He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَوَكِّلِينَ** "Indeed Allah loves those who trust [in Him]" (3:159); and He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ وَيُحِبُّ الْمُتطَهِّرِينَ** "Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep pure" (2:222), among other verses.

If you trace the verses explaining the effects of these attributes and the virtues that follow them, you will find a great many noble traits. You will find that all of them lead to the conclusion that their possessors are the inheritors who shall inherit the earth, and that for them is the ultimate abode, as hinted at by the verse under discussion: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ** "O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion." He, the Exalted, has said—and it is a comprehensive word: **وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوى** "and the outcome will be in favour of God-consciousness" (20:132). We shall explain the meaning of the outcome being for godwariness in its appropriate place, if Allah, the Mighty, wills.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ** "lowly towards the faithful, mighty against the faithless." "The lowly" (الأذلة) and "the mighty" (الأعزة) are the plurals of "lowly" (الذليل) and "mighty" (العزيز). They are metaphors for their lowering the wing of humility to the believers out of veneration for Allah, Who is their Guardian and they are His friends; and for their rising above any concern for the false might possessed by the disbelievers, which the religion gives no weight to. This is as He disciplined His Prophet in His saying: **لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them, and do not grieve for them, and lower your wing to the faithful." (15:88). Perhaps the use of the preposition "upon" (على) with "lowly" (أذلة) is to imply the meaning of tenderness (الحنان) or affection (الحنو), as has been suggested.

His saying, the Exalted: **يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ** "waging jihad in the way of Allah, and not fearing the blame of any blamer." As for His saying: **يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ** "waging jihad in the way of Allah," it was specifically mentioned among their many merits because the need for it is pressing in this context—to explain that Allah grants victory to His religion through them. As for His saying: **وَلا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ** "and not fearing the blame of any blamer," it is apparently a circumstantial clause (حال) related to all the preceding sentences, not just the last one—though the latter is the certain case in such constructions. This is because the victory of the religion through striving in the way of Allah is hindered by the blame of blamers who warn them against wasting wealth, losing lives, and enduring hardships and calamities. Similarly, being lowly toward the believers and mighty against the disbelievers—who possess the ornaments of the world, the objects of desire, and the enjoyments of life that the believers do not—are also matters hindered by the blame of the blamer. In the verse is a prophecy of the Unseen (ملحمة غيبية) which we shall discuss in a combined discourse of Quran and Hadith, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

## (Commentary)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ** (the verse): Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il*, and Ibn 'Asakir narrated from 'Ubadah ibn al-Walid that 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit said: "When the Banu Qaynuqa' fought the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul clung to their cause and stood by them. 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and declared his innocence before Allah and His Messenger from their alliance. He was one of the Banu 'Awf ibn al-Khazraj and had an alliance with them similar to what 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy had. He renounced them before the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'I take Allah, His Messenger, and the believers as my allies, and I declare my innocence before Allah and His Messenger from the alliance and guardianship of these disbelievers.'"

Regarding him and 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the verses in *al-Ma'idah* were revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ** until His saying: **فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ اللهِ هُمُ الْغالِبُونَ**.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Jarir narrated from 'Atiyyah ibn Sa'd who said: "'Ubadah ibn al-Samit from the Banu al-Harith ibn al-Khazraj came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I have many allies among the Jews, and I declare my innocence before Allah and His Messenger from the guardianship of the Jews, and I take Allah and His Messenger as my allies.'"

'Abdullah ibn Ubayy said: "I am a man who fears the turns of fortune (الدوائر); I will not declare my innocence from the guardianship of my allies." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy: "O Aba al-Hubab, do you see that which you begrudged 'Ubadah regarding the guardianship of the Jews is for you alone?" He said: "Then I accept." So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ** until He reached His saying: **وَاللهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ**.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn 'Abbas who said: "'Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul believed [outwardly] and said: 'Between me and the Banu Qurayzah and al-Nadir is an alliance, and I fear the turns of fortune,' so he apostatized as a disbeliever. 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit said: 'I declare my innocence before Allah from the alliance of Qurayzah and al-Nadir, and I take Allah, His Messenger, and the believers as my allies.'"

So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ** until His saying: **فَتَرَى الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ يُسارِعُونَ فِيهِمْ**, meaning 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy. And His saying: **إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ**, meaning 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit and the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He said: **وَلَوْ كانُوا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالنَّبِيِّ وَما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَا اتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِياءَ وَلكِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ**.

I say: The story has been narrated through other paths as well. It has already been mentioned that these "occasions of revelation" (أسباب النزول) are interpretive applications (تطبيقات اجتهادية), and they contain signs indicating this. For how can it be when the verses mention the Christians along with the Jews, yet in the story of Banu Qaynuqa' and what occurred between the Muslims and Banu Qurayzah and al-Nadir, the Christians had no hand, nor did the Muslims have any dealings with them? The mere mention of them as an incidental or routine addition is groundless. In the Quran, there are verses dealing with the state of the Jews in the events that occurred between them and the Muslims, and the actions the hypocrites took therein, where the Jews were specifically mentioned and the Christians were not, as in Surah al-Hashr and others. Why then would routine addition apply here and not there?

Furthermore, the narration mentions that the verses revealed concerning 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit and 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy are seventeen verses (verses 51–67), yet there is no connection between them such that they would be revealed all at once (firstly). Among them is the verse: **إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ**, and narrations from both the Shia and the Sunnis have reached the level of tawatur (successive transmission) that it was revealed concerning 'Ali (عليه السلام) (secondly). Among them is the verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ**, which has no connection to the story whatsoever (thirdly).

Thus, it is only that the narrator took the story of 'Ubadah and 'Abdullah, then found that the verses suited it to some extent, so he applied them to it. Then he did not apply them well, placing seventeen verses in the stead of three verses simply because they deal with the state of the People of the Book.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from 'Ikrimah regarding His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ**: "It was regarding Banu Qurayzah when they betrayed and broke the covenant between them and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in their letter to Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, inviting them and the Quraysh to enter their fortresses. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent Abu Lubabah ibn 'Abd al-Mundhir to them to ask them to descend from their fortresses. When they obeyed him by descending, he pointed to his throat, indicating slaughter. Talhah and al-Zubayr used to correspond with the Christians and the people of Syria. It reached me that men from the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) feared poverty and need, so they corresponded with the Jews of Banu Qurayzah and al-Nadir, leaking news from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to them, seeking loans and benefit from them; so they were forbidden from that."
